sentences.text: Yes.
[PAUSES] While [?
Inaki ?] was trying to break through the door, and we were hiding ourselves under the bed, I was six years old.
My mother begged me, asked me not to speak and not to cough.
In this way, she saved my life for the first time in that day.
[PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS].
But after [?
Inaki ?] left with his loot, [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] Germans started to climb the staircase of the first floor to round up the Jews as they did in Poland or in other parts of Europe.
They weren't petty criminals like [?
Inaki. ?] They were professionals.
And when they penetrated into our flat, the first place they went, went to the bedroom to the bed.
They turned the bed up, and the three of us were facing a couple of German heroes, fully armed.
I can't explain until now why they didn't shoot us.
We were on the floor.
They could have done.
So one of these glorious soldiers asked us to get up, go through the door, and to follow his instructions.
[PAUSES] My father was in the front, I was in the middle, my mother in the back.
And this I still can remember, blond, young, powerful, pushed my mother with the butt of his rifle down the staircase.
[PAUSES] She rolled down a full flight of the stairs.
She broke the knee.
And for the rest of her life, she suffered from this.
Then he directed us to the street.
And the three of us, with the hero in the back with his rifle, with hands up, we were herded to the concentration place.
[PAUSES]
It was quite a long walk, I would say about half an hour.
And if somebody tries to hold his arms up for half an hour, will realize how difficult it is.
What about a child of six?
From time to time, the German soldier, if you can call him a soldier, was shouting at me to keep my hands up.
And my mother-- I didn't understand what he was saying-- my mother was telling me, hold your hands up, [PAUSES] because otherwise he will shoot you.
[SPEAKING GERMAN] This was my first lesson of a foreign language, learned and the hard way.
I saw many years later the photograph of a child with hands up in Poland.
The only difference is that I didn't have the honor of a German artist to take my picture.
[PAUSES] But was no difference.
[PAUSES]
So in this way, we were driven to the assembly place, which was the main police station of Iasi.

As we were coming closer, more and more people were herded into the same place, the same direction from various streets, generally by German troops who were practicing on us for what was going to happen a couple days later in Russia.
And we were aligned in a long, winding queue.
It was like a long snake, like a serpentine.
And we heard shootings in the courtyard of the police station.
It was a wall.
People were taken by the tens, put against the wall, and machine gunned.
And the queue was progressing.
The line was shortening, but it was a long, long line.
And there were Romanian Christian [PAUSES] [CLEARS THROAT] Iron Guards taking the dead bodies and piling them up in huge piles.
I saw people there who weren't properly killed, and they were thrown like this, still alive.
[PAUSES] When my mother saw this, we were guarded on the queue by Romanian army officers.
The Germans, they took only the pleasure to round up the people.
They left the dirty work to be done by the Romanians themselves.
They didn't want to dirty themselves with blood and-- [PAUSES FOR 9 SECONDS] Next to us was a young Romanian officer.
My mother kneeled in front of him-- she was 28-- [PAUSES] kissed his hand, and asked him, officer, sir, I beg you, take this child and adopt him.
He's intelligent.
He will love you.
Please take him.
Kill us, but take this child.
[PAUSES] The officer said, Madam, [PAUSES] please don't be scared.
[PAUSES] I'll protect you.
And what he did, as the queue was winding, he took us from the position where we were and put us at the end of the queue to give us another chance, to prolong our life.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] And this was the second time when my mother saved my life in the same day.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Probably this officer was a man who had some sort of conscience, some soul.
He saw a young woman with a little child being exposed to such a terrible death.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Our good luck was that this mass execution wasn't organized by the Germans.
It was organized by Romanians.
If Germans would have organized this, everything would have gone according to the pogrom.
But Romanians are not that precise as the Germans, and probably the ammunition ended, was finished.
So until they will bring new ammunition to finish off the people, an order came to let women and children leave the queue.
[PAUSES]
So immediately my mother and I went off that place.
And we ran to my grandmother's house.
[PAUSES] The men were all cramped in the basements of the police, and they were locked up there.
For a number of days, we didn't know what was the fate of my father and others.
[PAUSES] So actually what happened was that out of the thousands of people who were there, [PAUSES] about 2000 or so were taken to the railway station and squashed in cattle [PAUSES] wagons.
The rest-- because there was no room in the train for all the thousands who were there-- were left to go home.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] I didn't experience directly what happened with-- I read afterwards the terrible thing what happened when these people were suffocated in those wagons.
Amongst them was-- there were two of my uncles and other relatives.
[PAUSES] And they were taken for seven days without water and very little air and lots of rotten bodies in the big heat of the summer.
And most of them, they died the most terrible death possible.
Some of them, they lost their mind.
And fortunately, my uncle, David, who now lives in Argentina, survived together with other couple of hundreds out of the thousands who were there.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] All in all, I was told by my parents that about 14,000 people were murdered on that particular day and the days after in the train.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] My memories are very sharp and clean from these days.
[PAUSES] And I can say one thing, that in one day, I matured.
[PAUSES] I understood what it means that you have to control yourself to hide, to protect your life and the life of others at the age of six.
[PAUSES] And I saw-- at the age of six, I saw death, executions, blood, piles of bodies.
[PAUSES] It's not an experience for a six-year-old child.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] So this was the story of that black Sunday.
We didn't dare to go to our house anymore, because we were scared that we'll face [?
Inaki ?] with his ax.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] We stayed in hiding together with the rest of our family in my grandmother's house, hardly having anything to eat for weeks.
And then my father returned, and the situation somehow improved.
Law and order started to be applied somehow, better than grab a Jew and kill it.
And slowly my father opened again his shop, but he had to move from that terrible place.
And we moved into a very small house close to the synagogue where my school was.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And then we started to spend year after year in war conditions.
Well, I started school after the pogrom.
The pogrom was in June, and the first day of school in the synagogue was in September.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] Memories from the school, they are very scant actually.
The first winter came, and the synagogue was very cold.
Was no heating.
So eventually, we put a makeshift tin stove, a small one.
And every child was bringing a piece of wood every morning.
[PAUSES] And the recognition for good marks was to be responsible to keep the fire alive in the stove.
And I was very proud when I got this prize.
It was written in my school report book that I was appointed as a supervisor of the fire in the stove.
It was the highest recognition.
It was impossible.
This was during the Second World War.
People weren't allowed to move anywhere.
There were rules and authorities, which were totally anti-Semitic.
They wouldn't let anybody move from one place to another.
You were stuck to your place and report to the local authorities.

In [PAUSES] June next year, [PAUSES] [CLEARS THROAT] as the Germans started to implement their Final Solution, the Romanians, [CLEARS THROAT] some people say under pressure, some of them, they say voluntarily, started to have a census of the Jewish population, what they called population of Jewish blood.
Not of Jewish [CLEARS THROAT] religion, of Jewish blood.
Because there were possible people who converted to Christianity or had only half of their parents of Jewish origin [PAUSES] who could have passed as Christians.
But it was specific in their law that anybody who has Jewish blood needs to be registered as such.
Now it was a big dilemma for my parents when they receive the forms to fill in, if to register me there or not, or to keep me in hiding.
Eventually, [PAUSES] all of us, we were registered.
And I kept this certificate of my [PAUSES] Jewish affiliation.
Probably the intention was if things were going well with the German conquest of Russia, to finish us off in one of the extermination camps.
But things went differently.
So we had a relative quiet life in Iasi after the pogrom.
I could go to school, albeit it was a bad location.
But the school was good.
The teachers were good.
We could go occasionally to a restaurant to eat.
So more or less, the life was quasi-normal.
What was the attitude of the general population to Jews?
I wouldn't say that it was [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] [CLEARS THROAT] too vicious.
They weren't friendly with us.
Some of them, they were legionnaires.
They were [PAUSES] extremists.
But the general population was mild with us.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Generally we could survive there.
And we survived.
And I couldn't complain that we suffered after the pogrom as people suffered in other countries.

We still had enough food, and my grandmother was a fantastic cook, was cooking for the whole family, and was preparing for the winter with all the jams and preserved fruit and vegetables, pickles and everything, and growing chicken and the geese.
And it was a reasonable life.
From this point of view, I can say that the Romanian population wasn't bad to the Jews in general, with the exception of the Iron Guard.

No, but actually I read in some publications that something really awkward happened, that the local Jewish community managed to bribe the Iron Guard, which is typical for the Romanian mentality, to be corrupt.
But to such an extent that an organization like the Iron Guard to be bribed by the Jews, that show quite a lot.
[PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] I was spending most of my time with [PAUSES] [CLEARS THROAT] school.
My mother-- my mother's ambition was to become a pianist, which didn't occur, because I didn't have the talent.
But she insisted a lot.
We had a piano.

She was playing piano and was educated by her family as a good Jewish girl that she must play piano and speak foreign languages, which actually helped us with translation she gave me from German-- from the German hero.
And [LAUGHS] we had a piano, so I had to practice and play.
So I did it.

When things went bad for the German army in Russia, and this started in 1943, and they got the big beating at Stalingrad, and everybody heard about this, and the Germans started to think twice if-- to [PAUSES] act against the Jews or the Romanians who were even milder than them, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS], everybody was thinking of running away from Iasi to avoid the fight, which will probably occur between the Germans from Romanians and the Red Army, which was a great offensive.
So then we started preparations for leaving Iasi.

[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] The situation in the country was havoc, because thousands and thousands of people of refugees were leaving the eastern part of Romania, which was at the border of Russia, to the south and the west to avoid the coming of the Russian Army.
And we were amongst them, with one difference, that we were Jews.
So first priority in the refugees' trains, which were overcrowded with lots of Christian refugees, [CLEARS THROAT] were sometimes [PAUSES] unavailable for the Jews.
So the Jews, they started with their normal tactic, bribing to get some place, secure a miserable little place for-- a compartment for 20, 40 people in a train, a crowded train.

[PAUSES] And this is what happened in the spring, March of 1944, when we managed to get a compartment for the whole family in one of the crowded trains through sheer bribery [PAUSES] of Romanian officers, because the trains were under Army control.
Where were you going to?
To Bucharest.
Our next destination was Bucharest.
